text: I’d prefer if we keep regional political discussions out of our community. There’s already enough of a presumption in software development that the world stops at the US border... I’m unable to escape American politics no matter where I look :confused:.

text: Hey guys, tried to put together a repo curating resources for learning Category Theory that I stumbled on during my exploration. Hope you find it interesting and would love to know if you have any feedback or know any great resources that I might have missed to collect: <https://github.com/prathyvsh/category-theory-resources>

attachments.text: Resources for learning Category Theory for an enthusiast - prathyvsh/category-theory-resources

text: It is American politics at this moment. But the original issue that Mozilla stated and asked to sign up for does not stop at any country border. In India we face a similar problem now. Tons and tons of mis-information. You just need to look out of your window and you will be amazed to see that the world is spread beyond the US border.

text: indeed, the syntax does fit on a postcard. And in GT, we have an interactive tool to get you through it, too: <https://twitter.com/feenkcom/status/1242760105013248000?s=20>

attachments.fallback:
<https://twitter.com/feenkcom|@feenkcom>: Explaining the syntax of a language is often done through static examples and lengthy text. The time has come for a new perspective. E.g., here we highlight the #Pharo syntax for a given method live. This tool can be applied to any method.

#MoldableDevelopment with #gtoolkit <https://pbs.twimg.com/ext_tw_video_thumb/1242759939728211968/pu/img/e5ldjt1mr3TLsxNL.jpg>
attachments.text:

text:
In any case, we do agree that as the environment gets more and more complete, our focus is shifting towards documentation of all sorts.

Just now, there is a course on Software Modeling and Analysis that is based on GT exercises: <http://scg.unibe.ch/teaching/sma>. It also includes a few demos.

text:
Hi! My name's Paul and I love the prompt "future of coding". In the past, I've worked at Facebook and led product infrastructure at Discord. I actually left my job to explore ideas in programming/tools/education and learn more about PL and other things.

Being interested in code/design led me towards frontend/product engineering. Having spent some time here, I feel the tools and levels of abstractions are great but have so much more potential. I'm excited for a day styling (css, etc) is mostly handled in the design tool. Working with code as text files seems primitive. I have many more thoughts and questions :stuck_out_tongue:

Some links: <https://blog.discord.com/discord-react-memory-leak-565c89763e8|Investigating React memory leak at Discord>, <https://bypaulshen.com/posts/monads-for-javascript-developers/|Monad tutorial for JS devs>, <https://twitter.com/_paulshen/status/1304855130748063744|Visualizing array.splice>, <https://twitter.com/_paulshen|twitter @_paulshen>
Excited to be here!
